coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence sensing probe based on functionalized mesoporous MOFs for non-invasive and detection of dopamine in human fluids
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Pan, Lei
Yang, Fan
Xu, Shihao
Lin, Dan
Jiang, Changlong
coredata.dc:description: Abnormal amount of dopamine (DA) in human body is closely relate to various diseases, such as Parkinson's disease, pheochromocytoma. Real-time monitoring DA is crucial for disease warning, diagnosis and treatment. Currently, most methods rely on invasive blood testing for detecting DA, which is only completed with the aid of the medical staffs in hospitals. Herein, a non-invasive fluorescence visual strategy is developed for the real-time monitoring DA, based on luminescent nanoparticles and modified mesoporous zeolite imidazole framework (ZIF-8-NH2) dodecahedrons. During the reaction process, DA is enriched through the spatial configuration of ZIF-8-NH2 and hydrogen bonding effect. The luminescence of Cr3+-doped zinc gallate (ZnGa2O4:Cr3+, ZGC) is inhibited by the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism to realize sensitively detecting DA. The intelligent sensing platform based on the designed fluorescence probe and color recognition system is structured for real-time detection of DA in urine. Furthermore, a skin-fitting hydrogel patch is prepared by combining a fluorescent probe with chitosan, which enables sensitive and accurate detection of DA in sweat without the complex sample pretreatment. The non-invasive fluorescence detection method provides an effective strategy for quantitatively monitoring DA in human fluids.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mesoporous dodecahedron
Dopamine
Hydrogel
Urine
Sweat

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Experimental Section
Materials
Instruments
Preparation of ZGC, amino-modified ZIF-8 and ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2
Effects of temperature, pH and reaction time on fluorescent probes
Fluorescence detection of DA with ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2
Selectivity and anti-interference of the fluorescence probe system
Detection of DA with smartphone devices and sensing platforms
Analysis of DA in human fluids samples by color change probes and hydrogels
Results and discussion
Synthesis and characterization of functionalized mesoporous MOFs dodecahedrons
The sensing mechanism of the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 fluorescence probe
The response to DA with ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2
Detection of DA in human urine
Non-invasive detection of DA with hydrogel in human sweat
Conclusion
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Acknowledgements
Supplementary data
References

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthesis of the color change of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 mesoporous dodecahedron. (B) Design of the probe for urine (left) and hydrogel for sweat as a wearable patch on human skin (right).

(A) FT-IR spectra of ZIF-8, ZIF-8-NH2, ZGC and ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2. (B) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of ZIF-8, ZIF-8-NH2, ZGC and ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2. (C) Powder X-ray diffraction pattern of ZIF-8, ZIF-8-NH2, ZGC and ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2. (D) The fluorescence spectrum of ZGC, ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 and ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 in the presence of DA.
SEM image of (A) ZIF-8 and (B) ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2, TEM image of (C) ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 with n[ZGC]/n[ZIF-8-NH2 at 0.4:1. (D) Mechanism of the color change of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2.

Fluorescence quenching performance of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 with n[ZGC]/n[ZIF-8-NH2] at 0.4:1 for DA. (A) Fluorescence spectra of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 in the presence of DA with concentrations between 0 and 4 μM. (B) Linear plot of fluorescence intensity versus the concentration of DA. (C) Selectivity of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 based the sensing system. (D) The histogram of selectivity and interference experiments.

(A) Schematic diagram of dopamine detection in human urine with ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2. (B) Color change of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 with adding different concentration of urine (The photos were taken with a 254 nm UV lamp); Linear plot of B/R versus DA concentration. (C) Fluorescence spectrogram of the of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 with adding different concentration of urine. Linear plot of fluorescence intensity versus the concentration of DA.

(A) SEM image of hydrogel morphology in the absence of DA and in the presence of DA. The mechanical performance of the sensor after tension, torsion and folding. (B) Color change of the hydrogel sensor with adding different concentration of DA (The images were taken with a 254 nm UV lamp). (C) Schematic of DA detection using smartphone color identifier. Schematic of hydrogel patch on human skin. Linear plot of R/B versus DA concentration. Fluorescence spectrogram of the hydrogel sensor with adding different concentration of DA.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence sensing probe based on functionalized mesoporous MOFs for non-invasive and detection of dopamine in human fluids

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, 230031, China.
Corresponding author. Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, 230031, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Lei Pan and Fan Yang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Abnormal amount of dopamine (DA) in human body is closely relate to various diseases, such as Parkinson's disease, pheochromocytoma. Real-time monitoring DA is crucial for disease warning, diagnosis and treatment. Currently, most methods rely on invasive blood testing for detecting DA, which is only completed with the aid of the medical staffs in hospitals. Herein, a non-invasive fluorescence visual strategy is developed for the real-time monitoring DA, based on luminescent nanoparticles and modified mesoporous zeolite imidazole framework (ZIF-8-NH2) dodecahedrons. During the reaction process, DA is enriched through the spatial configuration of ZIF-8-NH2 and hydrogen bonding effect. The luminescence of Cr3+-doped zinc gallate (ZnGa2O4:Cr3+, ZGC) is inhibited by the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism to realize sensitively detecting DA. The intelligent sensing platform based on the designed fluorescence probe and color recognition system is structured for real-time detection of DA in urine. Furthermore, a skin-fitting hydrogel patch is prepared by combining a fluorescent probe with chitosan, which enables sensitive and accurate detection of DA in sweat without the complex sample pretreatment. The non-invasive fluorescence detection method provides an effective strategy for quantitatively monitoring DA in human fluids.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Dysregulation of the DA in the human's system has been associated with the development of Parkinson's disease [1,2], schizophrenia and other disorders [3,4]. The abnormal presence of DA in human fluids is related with neuroendocrine tumors, adrenal pheochromocytomas, extra-adrenal sympathetic paragangliomas (PPGLs) and other various diseases [5,6]. The clinical symptoms of PPGLs are difficult to diagnose due to the exhibition of similar clinical symptoms [7,8], and the longer diagnostic period may reduce the effectiveness of treatment to some extent. Abnormal concentrations in the body and metabolic processes can lead to neurological disorders and premature death in the human body [9]. Sensitive and selective detection of DA is essential for clinical diagnosis and subsequent treatment. Therefore, DA levels have been used as an indicative marker for these neurological disorders. There have been many methods dedicated to the detection of DA, including fluorometric analysis [10], electrochemical methods [11], and highly performance liquid chromatography [12]. The detection methods for DA require complex sample pretreatment processes, expensive instruments and cumbersome instrumental procedures [13], so there is a need to develop simple, rapid and low-cost detection methods and testing techniques [14,15]. In contrast, fluorescence assays have received increasing attention due to their high sensitivity [16], selectivity, simplicity, rapidity and stability [17]. Because the oxidation products of DA have good fluorescence quenching ability for monochromatic fluorophores mainly through PET or the mechanism of Förster resonance-based energy transfer (FRET) [18,19], which can be widely used for the analytical detection of DA. However, due to the presence of proteins and other biological pigments, the detection of DA in biological matrices with fluorescence analysis can be interfered and affected by their autofluorescence or light scattering [20], and further affects the output of sensing signal and detection sensitivity.

Persistent light-emitting nanoparticles (PLNPs) have been introduced to solve the above issues. In particular, the past few years have witnessed the development of ZGC PLNPs with near-infrared (NIR, 650–750 nm) persistent luminescence for the sensitive determination of various targets [21,22]. On the one hand, for the purpose of the synthesized bulk crystals convert into nano-sized particles [23–26], the synthesis of ZGC PLNPs requires solid-state annealing with high temperatures and complex physical methods. In contrast, the ZGC nanoparticles synthesized by hydrothermal method can effectively eliminate the interference of auto-fluorescence and greatly improve the sensitivity and signal-to-noise ratio of biosensing, while having the advantages of chemical stability and low cytotoxicity [27,28]. On the other hand, luminescent nanoprobes composited with other functional nanomaterials can further improve the sensing performance [29]. Specifically, carriers with excellent loading capacity or special interactions with the luminophores [30], combined with nanoprobes with high luminous efficiency, the sensing performance can be further improved. Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) have unique advantages when applied to sensing platforms: firstly, MOFs have excellent molecular adsorption capacity, with a large number of pores serving as natural binding sites for target molecules [31]. That is, MOFs as porous matrices can pre-enrich target analytes; secondly, the Lewis acidic or alkaline sites and open metal sites in ligands may lead to interactions between MOFs and target, and the microenvironment within the MOFs can improve the response signal output of analytes [32–35]. Therefore, it is reasonable to assume that versatile PLNPs/MOFs composite integrated platforms are designed for ultra-sensitive target detection [36]. However, the pore size of most MOFs belongs to microporous (<2 nm), which hinders the mass transfer rate and the openness of the sensing channel to some extent [37]. Therefore, functional mesoporous MOFs composites will become potential materials with high target detection sensitivity.

We designed ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 with high sensitivity and selectivity to detect DA based on the fluorescence nanoparticles and amino-modified ZIF-8. ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 was modified porous nanocomposites obtained by co-precipitation of Cr3+-doped zinc gallate PLNPs with modified layered porous ZIF-8-NH2. The fluorescence properties of PLNPs, specifically, show purple fluorescence by emission at 454 nm and 700 nm under UV excitation at 254 nm. In the systems of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2, the final oxide polydopamine (PDA) covers the surface of ZIF-8-NH2, forming a black layer. With the increasing concentration of DA, the trapping ability of polydopamine to Zn2+ was enhanced with broken the coordination bond of Zn2+-2-MI, while the Zn2+ was simultaneously chelated by PDA, leading to the destruction of the ZIF-8-NH2 structure. At the same time, electrons are transferred from the photoexcited ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 to the oxidation product PDA, leading to the fluorescence quenching of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2. In this manuscript, it enables rapid visual detection of DA in human body and allows sensitive and accurate detection without complex sample pretreatment. Meanwhile, the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 fluorescent probe combined with a smartphone for the non-intrusive visualization and quantification of DA in human urine and sweat samples, and the results are compared with other detection methods, propose an effective strategy for quantitative monitoring of DA in biological samples to safeguard human health.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental Section
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 2-methylimidazole (2-MI), gallium nitrate hydrate, chromium(III) nitrate nonahydrate and chitosan from Aladdin Technology Co. Ltd. Hydrochloric acid, ammonia, 2-Propanol and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. DA was purchased from Aladdin Co. Ltd. Ultrapure water was received by a Millipore water purification system (18.2 MΩ cm−1). All chemicals are used of analytical pure and can be used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Instruments
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were performed on JEM-1400F. Zeta potential were tested with Marvin nanoparticle potential meter. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy was obtained with a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. Fourier transform Infrared spectra (FT-IR) were conducted with Nicolet iS5 FTIR spectrometer. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was the TD-3500 X-ray diffractometer. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence spectra was acquired by a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. The data of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were collect by a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 high-performance electron spectrometer. All photos were taken under an365 nm portable UV lamp by a Canon EOS 8R digital camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ZGC, amino-modified ZIF-8 and ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2
ce:section.ce:para: ZGC was synthesized with a one-pot hydrothermal method. In general, mixed 2 mmol Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 2 mmol Ga(NO3)3·xH2O, and 0.004 mmol Cr(NO3)3·xH2O and stirred evenly. Adding ultrapure water to 15 mL. Then, quickly added 28 % ammonia solution to make the pH at 9 to pH 10. After stirring for 0.5 h, pouring into a 50 mL reactor with Teflon lining at 220 °C for 12 h. Then centrifugated and washed with 0.01 M HCl. ZGC was mixed with isopropanol and centrifuged to obtain it. Finally, ZGC was dispersed in ultrapure water at a concentration of 4 mg mL−1. Took 2.0 g Zn(NO3)2·6H2O and mixed with 12.1 mL of ultrapure water, mixed 3.312 g 2-methylimidazole and 48.5 mL of deionized water and then 3.0 mL of triethylamine. Zn2+ solution was slowly poured into it, mixed well and stirred for 30 min, centrifuged with deionized water for three times, dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Then the ZIF-8 was obtained. Taken 1 g of dried ZIF-8 powder, added 25 mL of 25 % ammonia solution to ultrasonicate for 1 h and centrifuged after stirring vigorously for 24 h at 20 °C. After repeated washing with ultrapure water and drying at 60 °C for 24 h. ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 was synthesized by co-precipitation method. ZGC was redispersed in ultrapure water, 100 mg of ZIF-8-NH2 was added and stirred for 4 h. The powder washed by ultrapure water and dried in a vacuum at 50 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of temperature, pH and reaction time on fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: In this paper, a series of experiments were performed to optimize the assay reaction time, temperature and pH of the buffer solution to investigate the time course of fluorescence quenching induced by DA. The fluorescence intensity did not decrease further substantially after 10 min of reaction with 4.5 μM DA. Therefore, a time of 10 min was chosen for further experiments in this paper. Temperature is another important factor affecting the fluorescence activity of DA quenching, and the quenching efficiency increases with increasing temperature (from 30 °C to 45 °C), which may be due to the higher efficiency of intermolecular interactions at higher temperatures. Here, 37 °C was chosen for subsequent sensing accordingly in this paper in order to be closest to the physiological environment. In addition, in this paper, we tried to change the pH of Tris-HCl buffer (4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0 and 9.0) and finally found that changing the pH in this range had no significant effect on the quenching efficiency. Therefore, 2.5 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH at 7.0) was used as the final reaction solution and pH = 7.0 could also be used as the optimal pH to simulate the internal human environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of DA with ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2
ce:section.ce:para: The excitation wavelength was set at 254 nm, and the fluorescence spectra in the range of 300∼800 nm. 8 mg of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 was dispersed in 0.5 mL 2.5 mM Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH at 7.0). Subsequently, 2 mL of the probe solution was mixed with different amounts of DA solution, adjusted to 4 mL with deionized water. The suspension was incubated at 37 °C for 10 min, then used a quartz cuvette for fluorescence detection under a UV light. The photos of fluorescence with a color change were taken in a dark environment by a camera. Each sample was measured three times and averaged before proceeding to the next experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of the fluorescence probe system
ce:section.ce:para: For an excellent fluorescence sensing system, it is important to have good selectivity and maintain a selective response to DA even in the presence of other interferents. Therefore, experiments were conducted to verify the selectivity and anti-interference of the color change sensing system in detection of DA. Under the same conditions, Vitamin C (VC), glucose (G), Levodopa (l-Dopa), arginine (Arg), Lysine (Lys), β-alanine (β-Ala), glutamic acid (Glu)，proline (Pro), histidine (His), epinephrine (E), cysteine (Cys), glycine (Gly), leucine (Leu) and glutathione (GSH) were added to the probe solution, respectively. The concentration is 5 μM in Tris-HCl. Then the selectivity and anti-interference of the fluorescence probe system and the color-changing hydrogel for DA were evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA with smartphone devices and sensing platforms
ce:section.ce:para: The ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2-based hydrogel and fluorescence sensing system was designed to further continuously monitor dopamine changes in human fluids, using data interconnection of portable smartphone devices and RGB analytical software. The color information corresponding to the red-green-blue values (RGB values) of the fluorescence photographs can be obtained through the color recognition application (ColorDesk APP was download in App Store in the iPhone 14 pro) of a smartphone (iPhone 14 pro, MQ1C3CH/A). Therefore, the RGB values were analyzed to quantify DA. For the reliability and accuracy in the experiment, they were tested for three times to obtain the value of R/B before proceeding to the next step. The color change of fluorescence was taken with a camera in a dark environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of DA in human fluids samples by color change probes and hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 for detecting DA in real samples, urine and sweat were first collected from volunteers and diluted with Tris-HCl buffer. Then, samples were prepared by adding different concentrations of DA standard solutions to the diluted urine. The samples were then mixed with ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 at 37 °C for 10 min, the continuous luminescence intensity was monitored at 254 nm for quantitative analysis. Chitosan powder was dissolved in hot ultrapure water, continuously stirring and adjust the pH to acidity with acetic acid, until it was completely dissolved to obtain 20 mL of aqueous chitosan solution with a concentration of 2 g/L. Then, 200 mg of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 composite material was added and 1 mL of anhydrous ethanol was added slowly with constant stirring to make a homogeneous mixture of the hydrogel system. After that, it was poured into a glass cuvette and cooled to room temperature, and placed in a refrigerator at −18 °C for 20 h. After thawing at 20 °C, it was continued to be placed in a refrigerator at −18 °C for 6 h. After repeating this process for 3–5 times, it was placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C for preservation, and a light yellow slightly transparent sheet-like ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 chitosan hydrogel was obtained. By cutting it to a suitable size, the DA sensing patch was obtained by assembling the hydrogel and hydrophobic membrane. The sweat in the skin is gradually adsorbed by the hydrogel patch through the excellent moisture absorption. The DA in sweat is enriched on the hydrogel patch, and the purple color of the hydrogel patch in the chip gradually diminishes under the UV light irradiation. Using a smartphone, a 254 nm UV lamps and a hydrogel patch. A smart sensing platform was combined with hydrogel for detecting DA in human sweat. Then adhere the hydrogel to the skin to collect sweat during exercise. Under 254 nm excitation, the fluorescence images of hydrogel were taken by a smartphone, which analyze the photo and calculate the average RGB value with a color recognition APP for the detection of DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Synthesis and characterization of functionalized mesoporous MOFs dodecahedrons
ce:section.ce:para: The ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 was designed to realize a non-invasive and visual monitoring DA sensing platform in Fig. 1 A. Firstly, ZIF-8 was synthesized by zinc nitrate (provide of Zn2+) and dimethylimidazole in the aqueous phase, and further ZIF-8 will be aminated into ZIF-8-NH2, after co-precipitation with prepared ZGC nanoparticles, ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 was prepared completely. Under the UV lamp at 254 nm, ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 showed purple fluorescence, after adding DA, the purple fluorescence of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 gradually weakened, and the dodecahedral structure of ZIF-8-NH2 was disrupted with the increasing concentration of DA. Finally, the purple fluorescence of the system disappeared, and the degree of fluorescence color change was closely related to the concentration of DA. On this basis, we examined DA in human's urine and sweat (Fig. 1B). The fluorescence color change of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 in the urine system was analyzed by the RGB color recognition App of the smartphone, it was found that the DA of urine could quench the purple fluorescence of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2. At the same time, we combined ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 with chitosan to prepare a hydrogel patch. The hydrogel patch without contacting sweat showed purple color under the UV light, after contacting the sweat on the skin with an exercise, the purple fluorescence of the hydrogel patch would gradually diminished. The hydrogel patch based on ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 could be used for analyzing and monitoring the DA in the human sweat.

Initially, we prepared ZIF-8 under the condition of aqueous phase. As shown in Figs. S1A and B, ZIF-8 presents a structure of dodecahedral characterized with SEM. The particle size was uniform, with an average size of 200 nm. ZIF-8-NH2 with mesopores were prepared as a carrier matrix to load the ZGC, utilizing 2-MI as a functional ligand. SEM image in Figs. S1C and D present dispersed and dodecahedron-like ZIF-8-NH2. We also prepare ZGC with small size and excellent optical properties. ZGC can not only prove the enrichment on the surface of MOFs, but also enlarge the contact area with DA, the center ions located in the emission are all within the effective distance of interaction with DA. TEM was also introduced to verify the size and morphology of ZGC. Under the optimal condition of pH at 9.5 (Fig. S1E), ZGC has small average size of 6 ± 4 nm and well dispersibility (Fig. S3), finally ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 was successfully synthesized by loading ZGC on the surface and inside of ZIF-8-NH2 (Fig. S1F). The small size of ZGC not only can promote their enrichment on the surface of the host matrix, but also is conducive to enlarging their contact area with DA and ensuring all emitting center ions distributing within the effective interaction distance of DA. Then we systemically varied pH values (from 9.0 to 10.0) in the reacting system and compared the morphologies of the corresponding products. The results (Fig. S2A-H) indicate that the appearance and particle size of ZGC show difference with the different pH value. As shown in Figs. S2A and 2B, the morphology of ZGC nanoparticles showed an inhomogeneous lumpy shape at pH 9.0. As the pH of system increased during the synthesis process, the morphology of ZGC nanoparticles gradually became homogeneous and shifted to a granular shape in Figs. S2C–2H. When the pH of the system at 9.5, the synthesized ZGC nanoparticles shown through the SEM in Fig. S3 demonstrates morphologically homogeneous and exhibit uniform granularity under this condition. While the product obtained at pH 9.5 exits stronger fluorescence intensity than others. Accordingly, ZGC with small particle size and outstanding luminescence properties are successfully synthesized under the condition of Zn/Ga molar ratio of 1:1 at pH 9.5. TEM images in Figs. S4A and 4B shows the synthesis of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 with a large number of ZGC on the surface, confirms the mesopore ZIF-8-NH2 can enrich the small size ZGC on the surface. The large number of ZGC are adsorbed on the surface of ZIF-8-NH2 also confirmed the successful synthesis of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2. To prepared ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 nanomaterials, the negatively charged ZIF-8 is modified with NH3·H2O, due to the introduction of amino groups, the surface of ZIF-8 present a positive charge. Subsequently, ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 with electro-positivity were synthesized by ZGC and ZIF-8-NH2 to detecting DA through electrostatic interaction (Shown in Fig. S5).

The synthesized of ZIF-8 and ZIF-8-NH2 has been characterized in Fig. S6, the data of FT-IR, XRD and XPS indicated that –NH2 group was successfully introduced into ZIF-8. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy of ZGC at different pH and ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 at different molar mass ratio (n[ZGC]/n[ZIF-8-NH2]) were also analyzed (Figs. S7, S8, S9 and S10), optimized to select the best pH conditions at 9.5 for ZGC and synthesize the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 with n[ZGC]/n[ZIF-8-NH2] at 0.4:1. As shown in Fig. 2 A, both ZIF-8 and ZIF-8-NH2 showed C–H bending vibrations at 1465∼1340 cm−1, which was attributed to the presence of imidazole groups in ZIF-8 and ZIF-8-NH2. However, the N–H stretching vibration at 3500∼3100 cm−1, N–H bending vibration at 1655∼1590 cm−1 and C–N stretching vibration at 1420∼1400 cm−1 in ZIF-8-NH2 proved that the –NH2 group was successfully introduced into it. And ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 is also characterized by FT-IR. The characteristic peaks of Zn–N at 422 cm−1 and C–N at 1100∼1400 cm−1 in ZIF-8-NH2 are clearly presented in Fig. 2A [38]. Moreover, in Fig. 2A, the peak at 585 cm−1 of Zn–O characteristic vibration and 450 cm−1 of Ga–O bending vibration only existing in ZGC were found in the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2, respectively [39]. So the introduction of Zn and Ga element also proved that ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 was further successfully synthesized. The XPS of ZIF-8, ZIF-8-NH2 in Fig. 2B shows Zn2p and N1s draw into the ZIF-8-NH2, and Zn2p, N1s, Cr2p and Ga3d exist in spectrogram of ZGC and ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2, confirm the successful preparation of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2. As shown in Fig. 2C, the diffraction peaks of ZIF-8 at 2θ = 7.40°, 10.40°, 12.75°, 14.75°, 16.48° and 18.05°are consistent with those reported in literature. ZIF-8-NH2 is at 2θ = 11.02°, may be due to the degradation of CC–H on the imidazole ring during the modification process of N–H group, which caused by the rearrangement of the cage. The XRD pattern of ZIF-8-NH2 in Fig. 2C and Fig. S6D displays zeolite-type crystal structure characteristic peaks, confirming it matched to the pure ZIF-8 crystals. Besides, two sets of diffraction peaks of ZnGa2O4 and ZIF-8 in the XRD pattern of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 in Fig. 2C, indicating the successful construction of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2. The fluorescent properties of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 as investigated in Fig. 2D, ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 exhibit a strong emission peak at 454 nm under excitation at 254 nm in the spectrum, consistent with ZGC. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 decreased sharply after the addition of DA. However, the fluorescence intensity of ZGC decreased incompletely after the addition of DA in Fig. 2D. The insets also show photographs of the fluorescence under a 254 nm UV lamp, respectively. In the presence of DA, it has incomplete fluorescence bursting of ZGC, while the fluorescence of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 under the UV lamp suddenly extinguished, eventually almost taking on the dark color of environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensing mechanism of the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: The ultra-sensitive detection of DA was exploited based on the carefully designed ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 nanocomposite with induced inhibit of luminescence by DA, which showed purple fluorescence with emission at 454 nm under UV excitation at 254 nm. The PET mechanism inhibited the luminescence of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2, resulting in a gradual decrease of its emission at 450 nm, thus producing a significant color change from purple to lavender under a UV light. To clarify this mechanism, it's important to identify the exact inhibition agent, since PDA have previously reported fluorescence inhibit ability. Because the presence of oxygen in the polymerization of DA, we compared the change of fluorescence intensity of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 in Fig. S11 under aerobic and nitrogen atmospheres, respectively. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 decreases in the presence of 2.5 μM DA in an aerobic atmosphere. However, the fluorescence intensity displays less change under nitrogen environment. It can be concluded that oxygen is necessary for DA oxidation, resulting in the final adsorption of oxidation product PDA on the surface of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2, accompanied by fluorescence quenching. Oxygen proved to be necessary for the luminescence extinction, and as the core of the sensing system, PDA acts as an inhibition agent in the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2-based sensing platform. The burst effect is induced by a PET mechanism in which the PDA acts as a favorable electron acceptor, excited photoelectrons can be trapped immediately.

The sensing performance of probe depends on the fluorescence performance of ZGC and the participation of ZIF-8-NH2. As shown in Fig. 3 A and B, ZIF-8 exhibits a structure of dodecahedral, with modified by amination, the rough surface bundle of ZIF-8-NH2 provides a large number of anchor sites for ZGC to achieve enrichment of the target molecules in the ZIF-8-NH2. In addition, the specific structural of ZIF-8-NH2 may also improve the sensitivity of probe. With the addition of DA, DA is gradually enriched around ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 in Fig. 3C, the basic 2-MI in ZIF-8-NH2 can also make the oxidative polymerization of the surrounding DA to PDA. PDA has superior chelating ability Zn2+ compared with 2-MI, which may lead to Zn2+-2-MI ligand bond breakage and even ZIF-8-NH2 structure collapse [40,41]. We verified the morphology before and after the reaction of ZGC, ZIF-8, ZIF-8-NH2 and ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 with different concentrations of DA. In the experimental operation, ZGC, ZIF-8, ZIF-8-NH2 and ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 are added with 1 μM–5 μM of DA solution following reaction at 37 °C for 30 min respectively. As seen from Fig. S12 , the morphology of ZGC changes little even in a high DA concentration. It is calculated to be 2.34 ns by fitting the fluorescence lifetime curve of ZGC of pH value at 9.5 (Fig. S13). However, the binding of ZGC to DA resulted in the pathway interdicted for stimulate the electrons of the fluorescent probe with energy from photon excitation, the transport efficiency of electron in ZGC was decreased. The fluorescence lifetime curve of ZGC without DA was fitted and the final calculated result was 2.26 ns in Fig. S13, the decrease of 0.08 ns of ZGC with DA was indirect the credibility of the electronic transfer process. However, the morphology of ZIF-8, ZIF-8-NH2 and ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 have a shape change. In the process, ZIF-8 dodecahedra are gradually etched, leading to a little rough surface by the synergistic interaction between Zn2+ and PDA in 5 μM of DA solution (Fig. S14). In the system of ZIF-8-NH2 and ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2, the final oxide polydopamine coats on the surface of ZIF-8-NH2 forming a black layer. With the increasing concentration of DA to 5 μM, the growing polydopamine capturing ability to Zn2+ causes the broken of ZIF-8-NH2 structure (Fig. S15). Corresponding shape change at each stage of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 particles reacting with different concentrations of DA is displayed in Fig. S16. With the increasing concentrations of DA, ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 gradually became irregular, finally ZGC is separated from the broken ZIF-8-NH2. Because of the high surface area, mesoporous structure and excellent adsorption capacity with target molecular, ZIF-8-NH2 can be used as a pre-concentrator to effectively enrich DA in ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2. It indicates that abundant amount of DA can be combined tightly with the ZGC, greatly increasing the sensitivity of probe. The coordination bond of Zn2+-2-MI was disrupted by PDA, and the released Zn2+ were simultaneously chelated by it. At the same time, the electrons transfer from photo excited ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 to oxidized product polydopamine, resulting in fluorescence quenching of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2. Furthermore, it is calculated to be 2.64 ns by fitting the fluorescence lifetime curve of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 of n[ZGC]/n[ZIF-8-NH2] at 0.4:1.0 (Fig. S17). However, the binding of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 to DA resulted in the pathway interdicted for stimulate the electrons of the fluorescent probe with energy from photon excitation, and the electron cloud in the fluorescent probe is transferred to the PDA, the energy transfer efficiency of electron in ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 was decreased. Similarly, we have calculated the fluorescence lifetime at 2.53 ns of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 with DA in Fig. S17, the decrease of 0.11 ns of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 with DA was also clarified the electronic transfer process.

In summary, the probe has excellent sensing performance due to the morphological and structural characteristics of ZIF-8-NH2 and the small size luminescence ZGC. As shown in Fig. 3D, due to the large specific surface area, the small size of ZGC can be adsorbed on the surface of ZIF-8-NH2. DA around ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 can be enriched by ZIF-8-NH2 through the hydrogen bonding interactions, and more easily oxidized to PDA in the environment provided by 2-MI. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of ZGC was quenched effectively by the nearby PDA through the PET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The response to DA with ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2
ce:section.ce:para: The DA and PDA can be used as luminescence quenchers by the PET mechanism. Given the superior luminescent properties of ZGC and the mesoporous structure of ZIF-8-NH2, we considered using ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 nanoparticles as a probe to detecting DA accurately. Firstly, the quenching efficiency were compared of ZGC and ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 in aqueous solution with DA. As shown in Fig. S18, after added 4 μM DA solution under the same experimental operation, the fluorescence intensity of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 obviously decrease until the fluorescence is almost completely quenched, with the R2 of linear equation at 0.994. While the photoluminescence intensity of ZGC decrease and the fluorescence is incomplete quenching, with the R2 of linear equation at 0.973 (Fig. S19). It is obvious indicate that ZIF-8-NH2 can accelerate the quenching of ZGC by DA. Hence, the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 is considered to be the probe for accurately and sensitively detection of DA.

In order to obtain the probe of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 with excellent performance, under the optimal conditions presented in detail in Fig. S20，S21 and S22, we measured the fluorescence spectra of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 before and after the addition of DA. By linear fitting of the fluorescence intensity ratio to temperature, pH, and reaction time, we obtained the optimal experimental conditions for ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2, reaction with DA for 10 min at the condition of 37 °C and pH 7.0. Under this optimized condition, the response to different concentrations of DA with ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 was recorded subsequently. Fig. 4 A has shown the fluorescence intensity of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 gradually decreased with increasing concentration of DA. The color change from purple to lavender of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 with different DA concentrations also shown in the illustrations. With the increasing concentration of DA, the purple color of the solution under a UV light becomes dimmed and ultimately demonstrates observable black by naked eyes under the sunlight. The continuous generation of black PDA polymers around ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 may cause the solution turn to dark. Moreover, the concentration of DA in the range of 0∼4.0 μM exists linear relationship with the fluorescence intensity of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 in Fig. 4B. The coefficient of R2 is 0.994 and the corresponding limit of detection (LOD) is 0.075 μM estimated using 3σ criterion. Compared with several recent methods for detecting DA, Table S3 indicating that the method in the manuscript has similarly performance in non-invasive visual detection of DA.

Because of the complexity of the composition in human urine and sweat, we investigated several amino acids and substances with similar chemical structures with DA, verified ability of the selectivity and anti-interference of probe. As shown in Fig. 4C, ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 can only be quenched when DA was added. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I454/I700) of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 was enormously decreased in Fig. 4D. The interfering substances including Vitamin C (VC), glucose (G), Levodopa (l-Dopa), arginine (Arg), lysine (Lys), β-alanine (β-Ala)，glutamic acid (Glu)，proline (Pro), histidine (His), epinephrine (E)，cysteine (Cys), glycine (Gly), leucine (Leu) and glutathione (GSH), though the concentration was five times higher than DA, the effect on quenching efficiency can also be ignored. Firstly added 4.0 μM DA in the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 probe solution, other different interfering molecules were added to the solution subsequently, it can be seen the change of fluorescence intensity ratio (I454/I700) of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 is not obvious. The influence of interference substances in the system can be ignored for DA sensing. The results in Fig. 4D show that the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 has a good selectivity and anti-interference ability for detecting DA. The repeatability and stability of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 are also investigated later, ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 shows long time storage stability in Fig. S23. It shows that the fluorescence intensity of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 with DA and without DA remain unchanged for a long time. The probe has potential applications in DA sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in human urine
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the design of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2, we tested it in urine samples under optimal experimental conditions. The fluorescent photographs of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 infiltrated by different concentrations of artificial urine were taken with a smartphone, and the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 showed a gradual color change from purple to colorless under a 254 nm UV lamp as the concentration of artificial urine continued to be enriched in Fig. 5 A. We linearly fitted the B/R values of each ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 fluorescence photo, which resulted in a linear equation of y = 0.941x+1.080, where R2 was 0.968 in Fig. 5B. Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectra in Fig. 5C showed that the fluorescence of the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 at 254 nm gradually decreased with the increase concentration of DA in artificial urine, and the color change was also confirmed by Video S1. We linearly fitted the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of DA in urine, which resulted in a linear equation of y = 988.185x-233.451, where R2 was 0.995 (Fig. 5C), showing a good linear relationship, indicating that we can further analyze and process the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 by using the RGB software of smartphone. It proves that the DA detection platform based on ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 present excellent stability, selectivity and sensitivity for practical application. Thereafter, the sensor is applied to detecting DA in human urine. To evaluate the feasibility of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 for detecting DA in practical samples, we tested the recovery of the artificial urine samples using the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 fluorescence probe in Table S1. The recoveries of DA in urine by ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 ranged from 96 % to 107 %, demonstrated that the probe can detect DA for quantitative detection in urine samples. We extracted urine samples from different volunteers and used the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 probe for the detection of DA in real samples, futher compared the results with the HPLC-MS method in Table S2. From the results, it can be seen that fluorescence visualization detection method has highly accuracy for detecting DA compare with HPLC-MS standard method, and the RSD is less than 4 %. We also tested the collected urine of the recovery in the samples using the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 fluorescence probe in Table S2. The recoveries of DA in urine by ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 ranged from 90 % to 110 %. The recovery results further demonstrated that the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 probe can be used to real-time quantitative monitoring DA in human urine with a good sensitivity. We compared the fluorescence, electrochemical and colorimetric methods in detecting DA. The data were shown in Table S3, ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 probe has a low detection limit at 0.075 μM in the range of 0∼4 μM to DA, which can realize the sensitive detection of DA in human fluids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Non-invasive detection of DA with hydrogel in human sweat
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogels are often considered as highly hydrated cross-linked three-dimensional (3D) networks that can be endowed with various functions by tuning their composition and structure. Hydrogels are mainly prepared by incorporating MOFs into polymer precursor solutions, and chemical cross-linking of the polymer components around the MOFs can be achieved by free radical polymerization. The introduction of hydrogels improves the dispersion of MOFs and avoids the instability of MOFs, thus improving the mechanical strength, water absorption and total pore volume, and functional modulation of the hydrogel matrix. The composite material based on this response mechanism is cross-linked with chitosan molecules as a hydrogel in Fig. 6 , which is integrated with a hydrophobic membrane by cutting it to a suitable size, and the sweat on the surface of the human skin is gradually adsorbed by the hydrogel patch, with the DA in the sweat is enriched on the hydrogel patch, the purple color of the hydrogel patch gradually diminishes under the UV light irradiation. The patch becomes black under natural light by naked eye observation. The color of its color-changing hydrogel probe shows a continuous change from purple to colorless, and the degree of color change in the hydrogel sensor fluorescence system is used to detect the amount of DA in human sweat. Meanwhile, the wearable sensor of chitosan hydrogel can be used for the detection of DA in human sweat samples, which provides a theoretical basis in building a convenient real-time visualization detection platform with great potential for application in the field of chemical and analysis.

The ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 chitosan hydrogel shows better mechanical properties after the behavior of tension, torsion and folding (Fig. 6A). The SEM image of hydrogel in the absence of DA showed a better three-dimensional network structure in Fig. 6A. In the presence of DA, the three-dimensional network structure after adding DA showing except for partial collapse. Meanwhile, Fluorescent photographs of hydrogels infiltrated by different concentrations of sweat were taken with a smartphone, and the hydrogels showed a gradual color change from purple to colorless under a 254 nm UV lamp as the concentration of sweat continued to be enriched (Fig. 6B). Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectra in Fig. 6C showed that the fluorescence of the hydrogel at 254 nm gradually decreased with the increase concentration of DA in different sweat. We linearly fitted the B/R values of each hydrogel fluorescence photo, which resulted in a linear equation of y = 0.801x+1.147, where R2 was 0.971 (Fig. 6C), showing a good linear relationship, indicating that we can further analyze and process the hydrogel by using the RGB software of smartphone. Some reported hydrogel sensing patches relying on hydrogel chromaticity changes alone is susceptible to interference from ambient light and skin color, which can affect the readings of the detection results. Our reported sensing patch can emit light under UV excitation, avoiding the influence of ambient light and skin color. Under the 254 nm UV excitation, sustained luminescence of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 can also avoid the autofluorescence of biological samples (Biological autofluorescence is mostly at a 365 nm excitation), which improves the accuracy of the assay. We also performed spiking experiments on the artificial sweat and tested the recovery of the samples using the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 hydrogel patch. As shown in Table S4, the recoveries of DA by ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 hydrogel in sweat sample are in the range of 95∼103 %, the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 hydrogel is acceptable for the quantitative detection in human sweat sample. And we also tested the response of the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 hydrogel sensing patch to DA. As shown in Fig. S24, the detection signal (RGB value) stabilizes after the patch reacts with 4 μM of DA for 10 min. When using a ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 hydrogel patch to detect DA in sweat, the participants are required to wear the patch on the skin and exercise for a specific length of time before performing RGB analysis. As shown in Table S5, we used 4 μM of artificial sweat to wet the hydrogel after 15 min to test its RGB value. We considered that the saturation volume was reached when the volume of artificial sweat reached 50 μL. The sweat rate of human forearm during exercise is about 230 g m−2 h−1 and it does very much in different age groups. We determined the required wearing time to be 15 min based on the human sweat rate. As shown in Table S6, we put ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 hydrogel patches on the forearms of four healthy volunteers of different genders and ages. The volunteers in the trial ran for 15 min at a speed of about 6 km/h and excreted enough sweat to saturate the hydrogel patches. Subsequently, we performed RGB analysis of the luminescence of the patch using the portable sensing platform. The readings of the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 sensing patch indicated that their sweat DA levels were all significantly elevated. There was also a varying degree of decrease in purple fluorescence of the hydrogel patches in the photographs. The color change of the ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 hydrogel sensor can realize the quantitative detection in human sweat sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, we have developed a non-invasive fluorescence visual method for determination of DA in human fluids. A non-invasive fluorescence probe was constructed for real-time detection of DA by complexing fluorescent ZGC with mesoporous ZIF-8-NH2 dodecahedron. In this system, the pre-enrichment effect of ZIF-8-NH2 and the alkaline environment provided by 2-MI led to the oxidative polymerization of DA around ZIF-8-NH2 to PDA. Meanwhile, the photoexcited electrons were transferred from ZGC to PDA, quenching the fluorescence of ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2, and realize the sensitive and accurately detection of DA. ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 could be used for the detection of DA in human urine and sweat with the recoveries range from 95.3 % to 108.7 %. The proposed ZGC/ZIF-8-NH2 probe and its hydrogel patch prepared by combining with chitosan can be used for the detection of DA in urine and sweat. With the assistance of a smartphone sensing platform, it is expected to be a candidate material for the non-invasive diagnosis of DA-related diseases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Lei Pan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Fan Yang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shihao Xu: Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Dan Lin: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Changlong Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (2021YFD2000200) and Key Research and Development Program of Anhui Province (202004d07020013).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rechargeable and LED-activated ZnGa2O4: Cr3+ near-infrared persistent luminescence nanoprobes for background-free biodetection

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Autoluminescence-free dual tumor marker biosensing by persistent luminescence nanostructures
